summary: On Sunday, President Donald Trump announced a peace deal with Iran, authorizing the immediate reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and ending the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports.

The war, which began Feb. 28, 2026, with U.S.-Israeli strikes, resulted in more than 3,600 deaths in Iran and more than 3,700 in Lebanon, effectively closing the Strait of Hormuz and triggering global energy spikes.

Pakistan Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif , the key mediator, announced an official signing ceremony for Friday, June 19, 2026, in Switzerland, with mediators facilitating "pre-implementation discussions" this week to lay the foundation for technical talks.

Oil prices tumbled following the announcement as markets reacted to potential energy shipments resuming, while technical details on Iran's nuclear program remain deferred to a 60-day framework for further negotiations.

Sidelined from negotiations, Israel continued military strikes in Beirut's suburbs on Sunday, demonstrating the fragility of the U.S.-Iran deal as proxy conflicts involving Iranian-backed Hezbollah persist independently of the agreement.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the crash as a wrenching tragedy with broader safety implications, using words like “horror,” “brutal,” and even linking it to “one of the deadliest” skydiving incidents, while also hinting at maintenance and oversight failures.
Right-Leaning coverage is more urgent and casualty-driven, leaning on “BREAKING,” “mass casualty,” and “deadly,” and spotlighting the plane’s midair turn or turnaround as a suspenseful clue.
The dividing line is whether the event is treated as a systemic warning or a dramatic breaking-news disaster.
Both sides agree on the 12 fatalities and official investigation, reflecting shared concern over aviation safety.
summary: Twelve people died in a fatal plane crash near Butler Memorial Airport in Bates County, Missouri , on Sunday, authorities confirmed.
A pilot and 11 passengers were on board the aircraft when it crashed; all 12 occupants perished in the incident.
Multiple agencies responded to the scene, including the Missouri State Highway Patrol, Butler Police Department, and Bates County Sheriff assisting with recovery efforts.
Officials closed the area near Butler Municipal Airport for an undetermined time as the crash site investigation continues along Business 49 Highway.
Authorities have not yet released victim identities or aircraft details as the investigation remains ongoing; this is a developing story.
title: All 12 occupants dead in Missouri plane crash, state highway patrol says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts Trump’s Iran announcement as suspicious or destabilizing, using phrases like “fake peace deal,” “surrender,” and “nuclear threat” to stress political theater, escalation, and the fragility of ceasefire talks; right-leaning coverage flips that into a triumph narrative, calling it a “breakthrough,” “historical peace deal,” or a move to stop Iran’s nuclear program, while spotlighting the Strait of Hormuz reopening and even the $25B assets figure as proof of strength.
The sharpest dividing line is whether Trump’s timing reads as “strange” and premature or as decisive leadership, though both sides still note Pakistan’s mediation and Iran’s uncertainty.
summary: President Donald Trump declared on Truth Social that a landmark preliminary peace deal is scheduled to be signed Sunday, paving the way to immediately end the naval blockade and fully revive regional maritime trade.

"The Deal is scheduled to get signed tomorrow, and immediately after it is signed, the Hormuz Strait is OPEN TO ALL," Trump announced on Saturday, projecting immense confidence that the strategic choke point will be unblocked.

The White House's rapid timeline directly conflicts with statements from Tehran, as Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson publicly countered that a formal signing would likely not occur on Sunday, indicating that text adjustments are still underway.

Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, acting as a key mediator, mirrored Trump's optimism, stating that an agreement to end the war is closer than ever and "likely expected in the next 24 hours," with technical-level talks slated for next week.

The diplomatic breakthrough faces severe skepticism on the ground, following a Friday night flashpoint where U.S. forces had to shoot down several Iranian attack drones targeting commercial ships transiting the still-volatile Strait.
title: Trump says Iran deal will be signed Sunday, Strait of Hormuz to open immediately after

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the removal as a humiliating Trump defeat, using charged terms like “wipeout,” “losing,” and even “stupid name” or “illegal second name” to stress rebuke and legitimacy; right-leaning coverage instead spotlights the same event as a “controversial” change carried out in a “predawn operation,” emphasizing conflict, suspicion, and compliance with a court deadline rather than political embarrassment.
The key dividing line is whether the story is a symbolic takedown of Trump or a procedural legal act, with both sides agreeing on the court order and removal itself.
Center-Leaning coverage stays more procedural, focusing on the filing, timing, and “fully removed” status.
summary: The Kennedy Center officially confirmed Saturday that President Donald Trump 's name has been entirely removed from the building and its grounds, complying with a strict federal court order.

The center's leadership filed a formal notice of compliance around 11:00 a.m. EDT, with Chief Operating Officer Matt Floca certifying under oath that crews successfully excised all physical exterior signage that purported to rename the venue after Trump.

Workers carried out the removal under the shroud of heavy tarps during the predawn hours, preventing a crowd of gathered onlookers from witnessing the exact moment the 18 letters reading "The Donald J. Trump and" were unbolted from the marble facade.

The Trump administration simultaneously scrubbed the president's name from digital spaces, updating the performing arts venue's official website, erasing references from its YouTube and voicemail channels, and officially withdrawing all related trademark applications.

The legal resolution restores the venue's historic name—The John F. Kennedy Memorial Center for the Performing Arts—following a definitive ruling by U.S. District Judge Christopher Cooper, who affirmed that only Congress holds the legislative authority to alter a national monument.
title: Kennedy Center says it has fully removed Trump's name from its building after court order

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the strike as potentially “lawless” and “performative,” stressing legal and ethical concerns and reading Trump’s move as a broader hard-line power play; right-leaning coverage recasts it as a decisive win, using martial terms like “eliminated,” “take out,” “swift and lethal kinetic,” and “infamous” to celebrate toughness and border-security resolve.
The sharpest divide is whether the event signals extrajudicial overreach or anti-cartel strength, especially around the “terrorist organization” label and cooperation with Venezuela .
Both sides still agree the target was Tren de Aragua’s leader and a dangerous criminal, but they diverge on whether the story is cautionary or triumphant.
summary: President Donald Trump announced Friday that a U.S. military strike successfully killed Hector Rusthenford Guerrero Flores, the infamous top leader of the transnational Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua.

The operation was carried out earlier this week by U.S. Southern Command, targeting a Tren de Aragua compound located in Venezuela's southern Bolívar state with what Trump termed a "swift and lethal kinetic strike."

The mission marks an unprecedented, close collaboration with Venezuelan security forces, highlighting a drastic shift in relations since former President Nicolás Maduro was removed by the U.S. military in January and replaced by interim leader Delcy Rodríguez.

Trump shared unclassified, overhead footage of the airstrike on Truth Social, showing a small, green-roofed building exploding into a cloud of smoke, while asserting that the group's "terrorists no longer have safe haven anywhere."

The targeted killing provides "retribution" for American families, according to Trump, who dedicated the successful military action to the loved ones of victims of high-profile crimes linked to undocumented gang members in the U.S.
title: Trump says US military strike killed leader of Tren de Aragua gang with help from Venezuela
Wall St. Futures Advance over 1% as Markets Cheer Iran Deal - Regional Media News

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as a government-side “victory,” but keeps the earlier finding of “unlawful” and “discriminatory” front and center, suggesting skepticism about the ban’s legitimacy; right-leaning coverage shifts to a law-and-order lens, stressing “lawful,” “terror legislation,” “legal standoff,” and the ripple effects of mass arrests and prison penalties.

Center-Leaning reporting sits between them, using the court ’s “lawful” language while adding rights-based terms like “justified and proportionate” and context about targeting defense firms such as Elbit Systems.
Both sides agree on the ban’s legality, but diverge sharply over whether it signals security, repression, or civil-liberties conflict.
summary: On Monday, the Court of Appeal ruled the government's proscription of Palestine Action as a terrorist organization lawful and proportionate, overturning a High Court decision that had found the ban unlawful.

The government proscribed Palestine Action on July 5, 2025, under the Terrorism Act 2000 after the group conducted raids on Israel-based defense firm Elbit Systems, causing over a million pounds in damage.

Lady Chief Justice Carr rejected civil disobedience claims, stating Palestine Action "overtly promoted unlawful violence amounting to terrorism" and identified a "pattern of escalatory behaviour" justifying the ban.

Since the ban took effect, police arrested more than 3,300 people at protests, with campaigners alleging a "culture of fear," while the Chief Magistrate paused criminal cases pending a June 30 review hearing.

Palestine Action co-founder Huda Ammori announced plans to appeal to the UK Supreme Court, saying the ban imposes "severe restrictions on the fundamental free speech and assembly rights of vast numbers of people.
title: UK’s ban on Palestine Action under terror legislation was lawful, Court of Appeal says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a diplomatic breakthrough threatened by Israel’s refusal to leave “seized land,” using loaded cues like “won’t withdraw,” “rules out,” and “indefinitely” to stress occupation and possible deal damage; right-leaning coverage instead treats the same stance as hard-edged security resolve, with phrases like “won’t withdraw,” “defence minister,” and even “defiance” casting it as protection of borders and communities.

The sharpest dividing line is whether Israel’s move is portrayed as obstruction or necessity, while the U.S.-Iran “tentative” agreement and Lebanon’s warning to displaced residents provide shared context.
Center coverage stays more restrained, softening the clash into a “potentially challenging” procedural conflict.
summary: On Monday, Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif announced an interim U.S.–Iran deal calling for the "immediate and permanent termination" of military operations on all fronts, including Lebanon.

Defense Minister Israel Katz confirmed Israel is not a party to the deal, declaring the military will maintain its presence "indefinitely" and warning that Israel would retaliate with "great force" if Iran attacks.

Over the past two and a half years, Israel has taken control of 1,000 square kilometers across Gaza, Lebanon, and Syria, uprooting some 1.2 million people while razing villages and clearing the security zone.

Despite the ceasefire announcement, authorities in southern Lebanon warned displaced residents against rushing home on Monday, citing unstable conditions; many remain skeptical, with one stating, "Frankly, we are hesitant; Israel cannot be trusted."

Broader negotiations regarding Iran's nuclear program will continue over the next 60 days, according to Pakistani officials, while mediators facilitate meetings to "lay the foundation for the technical talks.
title: Defense minister says Israel won’t withdraw from land seized in Lebanon, Syria and Gaza

bias: All three perspectives rest on the same AP-NORC/AAPI Data poll, but they diverge in emphasis: left-leaning coverage foregrounds the Trump administration’s “heightened immigration policies” as a driver of “upheaval,” casting the decline in immigrant welcome as policy-caused; right-leaning coverage leans harder into the broader loss narrative, stressing that the U.S. is no longer the “land of opportunity” for immigrants, while still linking disruptions to Trump-era policy.

The shared phrases “no longer,” “upheaval,” and “land of opportunity” give the story a somber, decline-oriented tone, revealing a common concern over immigration and national standing even as each side frames responsibility and meaning differently.
summary: A new poll released Monday from AAPI Data and The Associated Press-NORC Center for Public Affairs Research found most AAPI adults say the U.S. is no longer a great country for immigrants.

Following more than a year of immigration crackdowns, the Trump administration's heightened policies prompted about half of AAPI adults to report they or someone they know faced detention, deportation, or changed routines.

Khoa Tran, 27, of San Antonio, Texas, began carrying immigration documents after social media posts emphasized legal requirements, with such identification becoming "literally a second form of identification in addition to the driver's license," Tran said.

Federal courts struck down policies this month that "categorically barred" immigrants from 39 Asian, African, Latin American and Middle Eastern countries from receiving asylum and citizenship decisions.

Despite these legal setbacks, 73% of AAPI adults say cultural mixing is vital to America's identity, though Soonho Kwon, 30, of La Mirada, California, expresses ambivalence about celebrating the nation's 250th anniversary amid immigration restrictions.
title: Most AAPI adults say the US is no longer a great country for immigrants, new poll finds

summary: President Donald Trump celebrated his 80th birthday on Sunday with a historic, unprecedented dual spectacle: announcing a major geopolitical breakthrough and hosting a live professional sports event on the White House grounds.

Hours before the festivities began, Trump announced an Iran peace deal is "complete," declaring that the United States will officially lift its naval blockade on Iranian ports and that the strategic Strait of Hormuz will immediately reopen to international shipping.

The "UFC Freedom 250" card was co-themed to celebrate the upcoming 250th anniversary of U.S. independence, featuring a military color guard, flyovers by the Blue Angels and Thunderbirds, and a massive crowd that included cabinet leaders and service members.

The night concluded past 1:00 a.m. Monday with a major upset, as heavy American underdog Justin Gaethje rallied to stop the previously undefeated Ilia Topuria in the fourth round to claim the undisputed UFC lightweight championship.
Polling from Reuters/Ipsos found only 16% of Americans considered the event appropriate, while critics dismissed the spectacle as a self-serving distraction from pressing national issues.
title: Trump Celebrates His 80th Birthday with an Iran Deal and UFC Cage Fights at the White House

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a peace breakthrough, using terms like “ending,” “war,” “peace deal,” and “cheer” to spotlight de-escalation and the market’s upbeat reaction; right-leaning coverage is more guarded, stressing the deal is “tentative” and leaning on punchier language like “skids” and “flagged” to underscore uncertainty and caution.
The key dividing line is certainty versus conditionality: one side treats the agreement as a meaningful step toward ending conflict, while the other treats it as provisional and anticipatory.
Both still converge on the same core market story—stocks up, oil down, Hormuz reopening—reflecting shared attention to geopolitical risk and energy flows.
summary: On Sunday, President Donald Trump announced a ceasefire deal with Iran, authorizing the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and the immediate removal of the U.S. naval blockade on Iranian ports.
Markets had been roiled by geopolitical risk since February, and the agreement provides relief to the global economy after nearly four months of conflict.
The Nikkei 225 surged 4.5 percent and the Kospi jumped 5.7 percent on Monday, while international benchmark Brent crude fell $3.45 to $83.88 per barrel in early trading.
Analyst Josh Gilbert cautioned that "the deal isn't actually signed until June 19th," with Pakistan stating the signing would be held in Switzerland.

Broader negotiations on Iran's nuclear program are expected to continue over the next 60 days, while experts suggest oil flows must recover to around 60% of pre-war levels to restore a supply surplus.
title: Tentative Deal on Ending the Iran War Sends Stocks Soaring While Oil Prices Fall

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump’s move as a punitive “threat” and “ultimatum,” spotlighting French wine “in crosshairs” and the summit’s diplomatic fallout; right-leaning coverage recasts the same move as hard-edged leverage, using “exclusive,” “warns,” and “no choice” to present Trump as forceful and justified.

The biggest dividing line is interpretation of the 100% tariff: left outlets stress escalation and harm, while right outlets emphasize retaliation against France ’s 3% digital tax on U.S. tech giants and, at times, the U.S. as a “major market” for French wine.
Both agree on the basic dispute, but they split over whether it reads as coercion or necessary pressure.
summary: On Monday, President Donald Trump threatened 100% tariffs on French wine and champagne unless French President Emmanuel Macron removes France's 3% digital services tax on American technology companies, according to the New York Post.

France implemented the 3% levy in 2019 targeting revenue from tech giants including Facebook, Amazon, Apple and Alphabet; Trump previously threatened 200% tariffs on French wine earlier this year as transatlantic trade tensions escalated.

The United States accounts for 21% of French wine and spirits exports, with alcohol products valued at roughly $10.46 billion in 2024 already facing a 15% tariff, compounding the proposed 100% levy's impact.

Gabriel Picard, president of the French Federation of Wine and Spirits Exporters , called the threat "bad news for our highly export-orientated sector," while White House and Élysée officials did not immediately respond to the dispute.

Trump issued the warning ahead of the Group of Seven summit in Évian-les-Bains, where leaders will address international trade policy; Canada previously scrapped its digital services tax under Trump pressure, establishing potential precedent for France.
title: Trump Says France Must Scrap Tech 'Sales Tax' or Face 100% Wine Tariffs: NY Post

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames ICE at polls as an alarming “voter fraud boogeyman,” stressing “siccing ICE,” “extreme lengths,” and constitutional danger, while right-leaning coverage recasts the same move as “defends” and “ensure only U.S. citizens vote,” emphasizing security, “threat,” and election integrity.

The sharpest dividing line is whether the policy signals intimidation or protection: the left foregrounds Trump’s “anything necessary” rhetoric and skeptical questioning, whereas the right highlights DHS reviewing voter records and the conditional “if a threat arises.” Both agree Mullin hasn’t fully ruled it out, but they split over whether that ambiguity is a warning sign or prudent caution.
summary: On Sunday, CNN's Kasie Hunt pressed Department of Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin regarding plans to deploy Immigration And Customs Enforcement agents to monitor upcoming midterm polling locations.

President Donald Trump has urged sending federal agents to voting locations, though Mullin claimed his officers would only deploy for a "specific threat" or as an emergency Special Response Team, not for intimidation.

Mitch McConnell and Susan Collins called the proposal a "non-starter" last week, while Mullin separately told Dana Bash he is ready to "throw out the Constitution" to ensure "only citizens of the United States are voting."

A study by the Center for Democracy and Civic Engagement found the proposed act would disenfranchise 21.3 million voting-age Americans, while the Heritage Foundation identified only 25 instances of non-citizen voter fraud.
Trump has urged Republicans in Congress to pass the act through reconciliation to bypass a Democratic Party filibuster in the Senate.
title: Mullin says ICE ‘only’ deployed at midterm polls if ‘threat’ arises
topic_full: Immigration and Customs Enforcement · United States

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a diplomacy-first “peace deal,” spotlighting the $12 billion release, “ceasefire” language, and even a “categorical denial” to show negotiation and de-escalation; right-leaning coverage turns the same dispute into a tougher, security-laden contest, stressing “pushing back,” “sticking point,” and the need for Iran to give up nuclear ambitions or reopen the Strait of Hormuz, while recasting the funds as conditional leverage.

Center coverage is more procedural, dwelling on “confusion” and “differing versions.” Both sides share the frozen-assets focus, but they split sharply over whether the agreement signals compromise or a strategic win.
summary: On Sunday, President Donald Trump and Pakistan Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif announced a completed deal to end the war between the United States and Iran, with the pact calling for immediate termination of military operations across all fronts.
Trump authorized reopening the Strait of Hormuz 'toll free' and ending the US naval blockade of Iranian ports, addressing months of shipping lane closures that had strained global energy supplies.

Mehr reported a memorandum stipulating $12 billion in frozen assets released before negotiations begin, but a senior US official rejected this account as 'spin,' calling it a 'pay-for-performance' deal requiring Iranian compliance.

The United Kingdom, France, Germany, and Italy expressed readiness to lift sanctions on Iran, contingent upon verifiable steps regarding its nuclear program and coordinated work with the US and regional partners.

Negotiations will span 60 days to address nuclear issues, with Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi noting the memorandum will be signed in Switzerland this Friday, requiring verification of both nations' commitments.
title: Iran Says It Will Receive $24 Billion in Frozen Assets

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the acquisition as a “dramatic move” to “expand its streaming footprint,” emphasizing strategy and industry shift, while right-leaning coverage stays tightly on the transaction itself, with little beyond the $22 billion “deal” and “buy/acquire” language.

The biggest dividing line is context: center coverage spotlights Roku’s “100 million users,” the “biggest prize,” plus “superior reach” and “monetization” potential, whereas the left and right de-emphasized that data-and-audience angle.
Emotional tone also diverges: the left’s “dramatic” phrasing is more interpretive, the center’s corporate-style superlatives are promotional, and the right is sparse and restrained.
All sides agree it’s a major streaming-media expansion.
summary: On Monday, Fox Corporation announced a definitive agreement to acquire Roku, Inc. for $160.00 per share in a cash-and-stock transaction, valuing the streaming platform at approximately $22 billion in enterprise value.

The merger unites Fox's premium live sports and news portfolio with Roku's leading streaming platform, combining reach to more than 100 million global streaming households into a scaled media technology company.

Existing Fox shareholders are expected to own approximately 73% of the combined entity, while Fox secured $12.0 billion in fully committed bridge financing from Morgan Stanley Senior Funding, Inc. to support the acquisition.

Lachlan K. Murdoch, Executive Chair and Chief Executive Officer of Fox Corporation, said the deal transforms the company into high-growth verticals while achieving approximately $400 million of run-rate cost synergies.

Anthony Wood, Founder, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Roku, called the combination an "extraordinary opportunity to accelerate our vision," with the transaction expected to close in the first half of 2027.
title: Fox to acquire Roku for $22 billion to create streaming and live content giant

bias: Left-Leaning coverage keeps the story procedural and restrained, stressing that Javice is “reportedly seeking” a Trump pardon, while right-leaning coverage sharpens it into a law-and-order frame with phrases like “fraud conviction” and “deal fallout continues.” The center adds the strongest judgment, foregrounding “after defrauding JPMorgan ” and even implying the bank “can’t be pleased,” making the legal stakes feel more immediate.

The key dividing line is how much weight each side gives to the fraud case and sentencing versus the pardon request itself; all agree on the $175 million Frank deal and the Trump connection, but they split on whether this is a political maneuver or a broader scandal.
summary: Convicted Frank founder Charlie Javice is reportedly seeking a presidential pardon from President Trump, with her camp quietly courting individuals close to the administration, according to The Wall Street Journal.
Last year, Javice was sentenced to 85 months in prison for defrauding JPMorgan after fabricating millions of customer accounts to inflate Frank's value before selling it for $175 million.

The administration reportedly weighs handing out roughly 250 pardons this summer to mark America's 250th birthday, attracting clemency requests from white-collar defendants including Sam Bankman-Fried.
Javice has backing from Apollo's Marc Rowan, an early Frank investor who testified on her behalf at trial and has since donated millions to Republican congressional groups.
President Trump previously sued JPMorgan and CEO Jamie Dimon for $5 billion, calling the bank's 2021 closure of his business accounts political "debanking," which JPMorgan denies.
title: Frank founder Charlie Javice seeks Trump pardon after fraud conviction: WSJ

bias: The left leans into skepticism, using terms like “FOMO,” “hits,” and “better value” to portray the rally as hype-driven and to steer readers toward other AI stocks, while the right stays with trading momentum, stressing “gains,” “record IPO,” and the stock being “on track” to rise, with concrete figures like 7.7% to $173.30 and Friday’s 19% jump.
The center bridges both by spotlighting the “biggest IPO ever” and the $2.1 trillion valuation.
The key dividing line is whether the debut signals a speculative rush or a market milestone, though all sides agree the listing was huge and the shares climbed.
summary: On Monday, SpaceX shares rose around 6% in premarket trading, extending momentum from the company's record-breaking Nasdaq initial public offering last week.
The stock jumped 19% on Friday, closing at $161 from an initial $135 price, pushing SpaceX's market capitalization above $2 trillion.
CFRA issued a 'sell' rating with a $115 price target, citing the firm's "extremely ambitious growth strategy," while New Street Research initiated coverage with a $165 target.
Morningstar analyst Nicolas Owens valued the firm at $63 per share on June 8, describing the stock as "overvalued" despite more bullish sentiment from other analysts.

Capital expenditures at SpaceX reached $10.1 billion for the three months ended March, up from $4.1 billion last year, as the firm prioritizes artificial intelligence following its February merger with Elon Musk's startup.
title: SpaceX Shares Rise in Premarket, Set to Extend Gains After Record IPO

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the deal as a risky, politically loaded “bet,” stressing “mammoth” size, “record” wealth, and Rinehart’s ties to Musk and Trump, while right-leaning coverage treats the same move as a savvy market play, using upbeat phrases like “snags,” “exceptionally led,” and “world’s hottest company” and highlighting collaboration in mining, agribusiness, and critical minerals.
The center strips away judgment, simply noting a large “stake” or “pours” money into a “record-breaking” IPO.
Both sides agree on the US$1 billion/A$1.4 billion scale and SpaceX ’s record-setting status, but diverge sharply on whether it signals power concentration or strategic opportunity.
summary: On Monday, Hancock Prospecting Executive Chairman Gina Rinehart invested more than $1 billion in SpaceX 's record-setting $75 billion IPO, marking the largest investment outside iron ore in the miner's history.

SpaceX shares surged 19% during their Nasdaq debut last Friday, pushing the company's valuation past $2 trillion as investors capitalized on Elon Musk's sprawling empire spanning rockets, satellites, and AI.
Rinehart described Musk as a 'truly exceptional' leader and praised his work with President Donald Trump's Department of Government Efficiency, or DOGE, to reduce federal bureaucracy.
Hancock CEO Garry Korte flagged potential 'mutually beneficial arrangements' between SpaceX and the miner as demand grows for materials needed to support advanced technology.
This acquisition expands a Hancock portfolio already holding Tesla, Nvidia, and Trump Media, reflecting strategic confidence in the West investing in technology and innovation.
title: Australia's richest person Rinehart takes $1 billion stake in SpaceX IPO, WSJ reports

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames glucosamine as a public- health concern, stressing “linked,” “speed memory loss,” and the 25% higher five-year mortality figure to spotlight risk for Alzheimer’s patients; right-leaning coverage turns the same study into a warning narrative, using sharper cues like “scientists warn,” “take by millions,” and “vulnerable patients” to amplify urgency and controversy.

The key dividing line is emphasis: left focuses on patient outcomes and caution, while right foregrounds broader alarm and scale; center coverage sits between them, highlighting “accelerate” and the disease-progression angle plus biological clues.
Both sides agree the supplement is common and the association is concerning, but they diverge on how sharply to frame the threat.
summary: Researchers reported in Nature Metabolism on Tuesday that a retrospective study of 65,000 patients found glucosamine use correlates with a 25% higher risk of progression from mild cognitive impairment to Alzheimer's disease .

University of Florida neuroscientists discovered the Alzheimer's brain adds excessive sugar structures to proteins through N-glycosylation; glucosamine crosses the blood-brain barrier and fuels this overactive system.

In genetically modified mice, the supplement significantly increased sugar attachment to proteins, causing deficits in 'social memory,' noted senior author Ramon Sun, Ph.D., of the University of Florida McKnight Brain Institute.

Matt Gentry, Ph.D., chair of the University of Florida Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, called the findings 'very provocative' but noted the study shows association rather than causation.

With more than 40 million Americans using the supplement annually, researchers hope these results will prompt investigation into metabolic dysfunction as a targetable pathway for combating Alzheimer's disease.
title: Glucosamine supplements may speed memory loss from Alzheimer’s, new research shows

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump as using “spy powers” renewal as leverage for a “controversial” voter-ID push, stressing the bargaining tactic and skepticism toward surveillance authority, while right-leaning coverage turns the same move into a principled stand on “warrantless spy power,” with sharper, more combative language like “refuse,” “won’t support,” and praise such as “asks the right questions.” The key dividing line is how the Save America Act is portrayed: a partisan pressure point on the left versus a reasonable election-security measure on the right.
Both sides agree FISA has lapsed, but they split over whether Trump is exploiting it or defending civil liberties and voting integrity.
summary: President Donald Trump declared Sunday he opposes renewing expired Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act powers unless Congress passes the controversial Save America Act, adding significant legislative hurdles to restoring national security authorities.

FISA Section 702 authorities expired Friday after Democrats refused an extension, citing concerns that Bill Pulte, the Federal Housing Financing Agency chief slated to become acting intelligence director, lacks the necessary nonpartisan standing for the role.

Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer demanded Pulte's removal Thursday, while Trump has struggled to secure enough Republican support to abolish the 60-vote threshold required to pass most legislation in the Senate.

Seeking a resolution, Trump nominated Jay Clayton, current U.S. attorney for the Southern District of New York, to succeed National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard as permanent director, with Clayton scheduled to testify before the Senate this week.

Pulte is slated to take over as acting director on Friday, creating a narrow window for the Senate to confirm a permanent replacement and resolve the intelligence leadership dispute before the interim transition occurs.
title: Trump says FISA extension must include voting bill

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as U.S. force under scrutiny, spotlighting phrases like “opened fire” and “U.S. blockade of Iran ” to emphasize militarized overreach and the weight of “dozens of warnings.” Right-leaning coverage instead foregrounds justification, using language like “ignored,” “circumvent the blockade,” and “being attacked” to portray the tanker as noncompliant and the response as defensive.
Center coverage is more procedural, stressing the reported sequence and anonymous sourcing.

The sharpest divide is whether the warnings signal restraint or legitimize force; both sides agree on the repeated warnings and flare/flyover escalation, reflecting broader splits over security, sovereignty, and state power.
summary: On Wednesday, the American military disabled the Palau-flagged Settebello after it attempted to breach the United States blockade of Iran in the Gulf of Oman.
The ship had dismissed nearly 60 verbal warnings and eight shows of force over two weeks, with a United States official characterizing the tanker as a 'shadow fleet' vessel evading sanctions.
Central Command gave the crew 15 minutes to vacate the engine room before American aircraft fired 'precision munitions' to disable the vessel, according to military statements.
Three Indian sailors died during the incident in the Gulf of Oman, prompting New Delhi to lodge a 'strong protest' with the United States over the strike.

On Friday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio told his Indian counterpart that commercial vessels must comply with United States forces in the Strait, emphasizing the blockade has been enforced for more than 60 days.
title: Disabled oil tanker received dozens of warnings before US opened fire, AP source says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the White House UFC card as a storm-battered spectacle, leaning on words like “chaotic,” “threatened,” and even mosquitoes to stress disruption, while also spotlighting Trump’s birthday tie-in and large crowd numbers as part of a politicized show.

Right-Leaning coverage is more celebratory or operational: it emphasizes the event’s “grand entrance,” lightning-delay protocols, and the seven-fight structure, though one item pivots to “health fears” over a bruise.
Center-Leaning coverage stays restrained, stressing the event was simply underway after a delay and noting Trump and Dana White opening the show.
All three agree weather forced a pause, but differ on whether it was nuisance, theater, or routine logistics.
summary: Weather forecasts raised concerns about thunderstorms and strong winds ahead of the UFC event on the White House South Lawn, creating the possibility of delays to the scheduled start time.
UFC officials prepared contingency plans for adverse weather, with lightning considered the primary factor that could force pauses or postponements during the outdoor event.
Organizers indicated the event would proceed if conditions allowed, but ongoing weather monitoring could result in schedule adjustments to ensure fighter and spectator safety.
title: UFC event at the White House underway after weather delay

summary: On Thursday, researchers published a study in Science identifying the physical mechanism behind the Venus flytrap's snap, revealing rapid softening of outer cell walls drives the closure.

For over a century, scientists including Charles Darwin believed water movement forced the trap shut, but researchers at Aix-Marseille University used high-speed imaging to disprove this long-standing theory.
When an insect touches specialized trigger hairs twice, the plant's 'internal motor' drives the leaf across an instability threshold, sealing the trap shut in as little as one tenth of a second.
Physicist Jeongeun Ryu suggested this mechanism could inspire soft robotic systems or adaptive materials that remain stable for long periods, then produce very rapid movements on demand.

Plant physiologist Professor Shabala remains skeptical, citing alternative water transport theories that could explain the speed, while researchers acknowledge future studies must determine how the plant controls the softening.
title: Venus flytrap's snap may come from rapid cell wall softening, not water flow: study

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Obama’s remarks as a warning against militarism, using sharp, anti-hawkish language like “bomb our way to solutions” and “step backward” to stress diplomacy and portray Trump’s approach as inferior.

Right-Leaning coverage keeps the same anti-war core but leans into skepticism and policy contrast, foregrounding words like “doubtful,” “framework,” and even “outright war” to present the debate as a sober comparison with higher stakes.
The key dividing line is whether the story is about rejecting force or measuring continuity with the 2015 JCPOA, which the right more often contextualizes.
Both sides agree diplomacy is central, reflecting broader ideological divides over intervention and restraint.
summary: In an ABC News interview released Sunday, former President Barack Obama stated that any potential deal between President Donald Trump and Iran will likely mirror the 2015 nuclear pact, calling it "doubtful" to mark a "significant improvement."

Obama's administration negotiated the original 2015 nuclear accord 11 years ago, which imposed restrictions on Iran's nuclear program for sanctions relief; Trump withdrew the United States from that agreement in 2018, launching a "maximum pressure" campaign on Tehran.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth argued Sunday that military pressure forced Iran to the negotiating table, while Obama cautioned that Washington cannot "just bully our way or bomb our way to solutions" instead of engaging in comprehensive diplomacy.

Trump claimed a deal could be signed Sunday to block Iran's nuclear capabilities and open the Strait of Hormuz, though Tehran has not confirmed it will sign, stating there is "no point" in current talks.
Obama suggested that negotiating deals solving 80% or 90% of a problem is preferable to war, noting the current difficulties serve as a reminder to prioritize diplomacy over military force.
title: Obama Says Trump Iran Deal Will Not Improve on His Pact

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as Trump’s deal colliding with Israeli defiance, stressing Netanyahu’s silence, “far-right” Ben-Gvir, and loaded phrases like “doesn’t bind” and “colony of the US,” while right-leaning pieces cast it as firm sovereignty and security , using steadier terms such as “memorandum,” “peace deal,” and “will not withdraw” to spotlight decisive leadership and a “fragile truce.” The sharpest fault lines are Lebanon’s clause, Israel’s withdrawal, and whether the agreement protects “our security” or signals pressure.
Both sides agree Israel rejects the deal, but they diverge on whether that is principled resistance or hardline confrontation.
summary: On Monday, Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir rejected the US-Iran peace deal, asserting that Israel is a sovereign nation and not bound by Washington's agreements.

Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi confirmed Friday that the memorandum was finalized in Switzerland, incorporating Tehran's key positions and including a $300 billion rebuild covering sanctions and Hormuz.

Ben-Gvir insisted that Israel must not withdraw from captured Lebanese territory, stating, 'We must not withdraw from any territory that our fighters have captured,' and demanding total dismantling of Hezbollah.
President Donald Trump criticized Israel's strike on Beirut, stating the attack 'should not disrupt this important process' and urging all sides to refrain from extending hostilities.
Referencing past agreements like the Oslo Accords, Ben-Gvir warned against succumbing to international pressure, insisting Israel will not lower its gaze before enemies again.
title: Strait of Hormuz: Trump's Agreement Doesn't Bind Us - Israel's Ben Gvir Slams US-Iran Deal

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds Trump’s jab at Netanyahu—“very difficult,” “very thankful”—and casts the Iran deal mainly as a way to blunt a nuclear threat, while right-leaning coverage turns the same remarks into a tougher security-and-diplomacy story, stressing “could resume attacks,” “slams,” and even the deal’s wider payoff for the Strait of Hormuz and frozen assets.

The center leans into the spoiler angle with “almost derailed” and expands the geopolitical frame through “Guardian of the Middle East .” The sharpest dividing lines are Netanyahu’s role, the meaning of the deal, and whether military warnings signal brinkmanship or deterrence; both sides still agree Trump criticized Netanyahu and tied the pact to Israel’s security.
summary: US President Donald Trump criticized Israeli strikes in southern Beirut as harmful to a near-final US-Iran peace deal and urged Israel and Hezbollah to cease attacks to support peace efforts.

Iranian negotiator Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf accused the US of lacking will or ability to fulfill its commitments following Israeli attacks, complicating Iran's position on the deal and expressing caution about its timing.

The deal reportedly includes the release of $25 billion in frozen Iranian assets, the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz without tolls, suspension of the US naval blockade, and Iran's agreement to halt nuclear weapons development with ongoing negotiations on uranium enrichment.
Trump declared the deal nearly complete, authorized reopening the Strait of Hormuz, removal of the US naval blockade, and warned of renewed US military actions if Iran fails to finalize the accord.
title: Trump Says Netanyahu Almost Derailed US-Iran Agreement, Should Thank US for Reaching Deal

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the London expo as a broader settlement scandal, using charged terms like “land grab,” “stolen Palestinian land,” and “illegally occupied” while spotlighting officials, activists, and pressure on the UK to ban it; the right-leaning piece is no less condemnatory, but tightens the focus into a sharper moral demand that it “must be cancelled,” adding a “secret is now out” edge.
The key divide is emphasis: the left leans into legal and political context, including organizer claims about borders, while the right stresses concealment and immediate action.
Both converge on the event as tied to occupied West Bank settlements, reflecting shared anti-settlement outrage.
summary: The 'Great Israeli Real Estate Event' takes place in London today, Sunday, June 14, 2026, amid intense scrutiny over its promotion of property in settlements considered illegal under international law within the occupied West Bank.

London Mayor Sadiq Khan condemned the expo on Friday, calling settlements 'unjustifiable and illegal under international law,' while the Palestinian Youth Movement mobilized over 100 organizations demanding cancellation.

The International Centre of Justice for Palestinians , a UK-based legal group, issued a notice to the venue citing potential breaches of the Fraud Act 2006 and Serious Crime Act 2007 related to settlement-based property sales.

Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper stated on Tuesday the government is 'pursuing this particular event,' while Khan confirmed the Metropolitan Police will assess allegations of criminality regarding unlawful property sales.

Around 700,000 settlers live in West Bank outposts considered illegal under international law, and the UK government has repeatedly affirmed settlement expansion violates international law, raising concerns the expo normalizes this enterprise.
title: Sadiq Khan Condemns London Real Estate Event Over Settlement Sales

bias: Left coverage frames the Geneva protests as a broad anti-G7, anti-capitalist, feminist, and anti-imperialist mobilization, stressing slogans like “meeting of the rich,” while right coverage recasts the same scene as “violent,” “far left,” and “chaotic,” spotlighting a “fortress” city, “military base” imagery, and fears of a repeat of 2003.
Both note the Tesla fire, smashed windows, heavy security, and Trump’s presence, but the left de-emphasizes disorder and the right de-emphasizes protest coalition politics and summit context.
Center coverage stays more reportorial, balancing size, security, and clashes.
summary: On Sunday, June 14, 2026, an estimated 20,000 demonstrators marched through Geneva to protest the G7 summit, which opens Monday in nearby Evian-les-Bains, France.
Swiss and French authorities deployed 4,000 troops and 13,000 police to secure the border, closing 25 of 35 road crossings to prevent a repeat of 2003-style rioting that caused millions in damage.
While the march was largely peaceful, a splinter group of 600 so-called "Black Bloc" militants set a parked Tesla ablaze and smashed windows at a bank and United Nations agency.
Geneva police deployed tear gas and water cannons to disperse protesters, reporting several arrests by Sunday evening, though officials stated no injuries had been reported so far.
U.S. President Donald Trump and other leaders arrive in Evian on Monday for the three-day summit to discuss the Middle East, Ukraine, and global economic imbalances.
title: Anti-G7 Protesters Clash with Police in Geneva

bias: Left and center coverage frames the bill as a labor-policy threat, emphasizing a “government takeover” that could “erode” employee and employer rights, while right-leaning coverage turns that same concern into a sharper ideological attack, using phrases like “forced unionism,” “unelected bureaucrats,” and a pointed question about Republicans backing it.
The dividing line is whether the measure is portrayed as a procedural effort to speed first-time contracts or as a broader power grab tied to the PRO Act and federal overreach.
All sides still converge on the same core tension: efficiency versus autonomy, with labor rights at the center of the dispute.
summary: The Faster Labor Contracts Act aims to speed up first-time union contracts but may reduce rights for both employees and employers, according to a labor policy group.
The act, supported by Democrats and 20 House Republicans, requires government intervention if no union contract is signed within 120 days.
If no agreement is reached in time, a government panel will impose a collective bargaining agreement instead of negotiation between the employer and employees.

Vinnie Vernuccio, President of the Institute for the American Worker, criticized the bill as gross government overreach that denies workers a vote on contracts and forces them to pay unions without input.
title: Group Warns Labor Bill Allows Govt Takeover of Union Contract Negotiations

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as coercive government overreach, using phrases like “arrive unannounced” and “intimidation tactic” to spotlight ICE as threatening migrant-child advocates; right-leaning coverage instead broadens the lens, presenting the same office visits as part of a “campaign to intimidate” legal services while stressing the children’s “special vulnerability” and the government’s long-standing role in funding help.
The dividing line is whether the event is portrayed as intimidation or routine enforcement with humanitarian context.
Both sides agree ICE and HHS tried to enter aid offices, but they part ways on tone—left more alarmed, right more justificatory.
summary: Agents from Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Health and Human Services visited Washington-area offices of legal aid groups on Wednesday and Thursday, seeking financial records without warrants in what organizations described as a campaign of intimidation.

The Trump administration has recently attempted to cut funding for non-governmental legal groups providing aid to unaccompanied migrant children, a move advocates describe as part of a broader, systematic attack on immigrant children's legal rights.

Michael Lukens, head of Amica, stated agents visited Thursday without warrants, while KIND reports the government owes $20 million for completed services, straining organizations that have represented 14,000 immigrant children since their founding.

Paula Fitzgerald, executive director at Ayuda, said agents requested billing invoices Wednesday, prompting her to require written correspondence, while the HHS Office of Inspector General stated they generally do not confirm or deny investigations.

Legal protections for migrant children date back to the Trafficking Victims Protection Act of 2008, which facilitated representation for children in deportation proceedings, though critics argue recent documentation requirements have created a 'paperwork barrier'.
title: Legal groups that help migrant children say law enforcement visits were attempt at intimidation

bias: Left-Leaning coverage broadens the story into a wider geopolitical tableau, pairing the calls with drone strikes, tanker detention, and even Iran, while emphasizing Zelenskyy’s hopeful “good ideas” to “bring peace closer”; right-leaning coverage strips it down to Trump’s forceful role, using sharper cues like “demand” and “reiterates” to frame him as pressing both leaders to end the war.
Center coverage sits between them, stressing the “rages on” urgency, bilateral U.S.-Russia relations, and follow-on diplomacy.
The key fault lines are scope and tone: Ukraine -only pressure versus global linkage and cautious peace-making, with shared focus on Trump, the G7 timing, and ending the war.
summary: President Donald Trump held separate phone calls with Russian President Vladimir Putin and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy on Sunday, discussing the war in Ukraine ahead of the G7 summit in France.
The calls followed Ukrainian drone strikes on Sunday that killed one person in Oryol and ignited fires at fuel storage facilities 440 miles from the Ukrainian border.
Zelenskyy confirmed his forces struck an oil facility to degrade resources funding Russia's invasion, while regional officials reported nine wounded in the Oryol residential strike overnight.

Separately, British armed forces detained the tanker Smyrtos in the English Channel on Sunday, which British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said is suspected of belonging to a "shadow fleet" violating international sanctions.

U.S. envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner will visit Russia soon, according to Russian presidential aide Yuri Ushakov, while further discussions on the conflict are expected at the G7 summit in France this week.
title: Putin, Zelenskyy speak with Trump by phone as drone strikes kill 2 in Russia and UK detains tanker

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as an accountability reckoning, spotlighting Melinda French Gates’s claim that Epstein was “pure evil,” “abhorrent,” and could have been stopped, while uniquely linking his contacts to Bill Gates to the divorce.
Right-Leaning coverage shifts toward drama and suffering, using phrases like “almost aborted,” “fights with tears,” and “suffers” to turn the interview into a personal ordeal.
Center-Leaning pieces sit between these, retaining the “visceral reaction” language but adding congressional context.
Both sides agree on Epstein’s notoriety, but diverge over whether the story is chiefly about systemic failure, elite scandal, or emotional trauma.
summary: In an interview published Saturday, philanthropist Melinda French Gates claimed Jeffrey Epstein "could have been stopped" had the U.S. justice system done its job preventing his crimes.

Her comments follow testimony by Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates before the House Oversight Committee this week, where he claimed Epstein attempted to blackmail him with details of extramarital affairs.
French Gates described Epstein as "evil" after one meeting and cited her 2021 divorce from Gates after 27 years of marriage as partly due to his unfaithfulness and contact with the sex offender.
Advocating for women in leadership, French Gates committed $215 million this month to women's healthcare initiatives, positioning her as a major funder in the gender equity space.
French Gates emphasized that transparency is essential for justice for Epstein's victims, while lamenting the degradation of women's rights in the U.S. political climate.
title: Melinda Gates tells interviewer ‘Epstein could have been stopped’ days after her ex testified to Congressional panel

bias: Left-Leaning coverage leans into the “Florida man” novelty and seriousness cues, spotlighting the BAC at 0.177 and phrases like “flew past” to stress recklessness, while right-leaning coverage frames the same incident more like a police -led enforcement story, using “troopers,” “allegedly,” and “on suspicion of” to emphasize process and caution.
The biggest divide is whether the 34 open White Claws and 90+ mph speed read as sensational misconduct or simply a charge under investigation.
Both sides agree on the core facts, but they diverge on tone: ridicule and alarm versus law-and-order restraint.
summary: On Friday night, Florida Highway Patrol troopers arrested 33-year-old Conor William Parady for driving under the influence after stopping his Honda Civic for traveling over 90 mph on Interstate 75 in Pasco County.
Investigators searching the vehicle discovered 34 open White Claws inside, with a photo released by FHP showing empty containers on the passenger side.

While speaking with the driver, the trooper noted Parady appeared heavily intoxicated; he later recorded a blood alcohol concentration of 0.177% at Pasco County Jail, more than twice Florida's legal limit of 0.08%.
No injuries were reported in connection with the incident, and authorities transported and booked the San Antonio resident into Pasco County Jail following the roadside arrest.
Patrolling near the 280-mile marker, a state trooper observed the speeding motorist at approximately 10:46 p.m. before initiating the stop that led to the arrest.
title: Florida Man Arrested with 34 Open Cans of White Claw in His Car During Traffic Stop

bias: Left-Leaning coverage broadens the story, stressing the docuseries’ wider context—successes, controversies, and contestant allegations—while using charged terms like “defamatory,” “bombshell,” and “surgical manipulation” to frame Netflix as potentially exploitative.

Right-Leaning coverage narrows in on the lawsuit and Banks as creator/longtime host, leaning on adversarial phrases like “manipulated,” “false and damaging narrative,” and “defamation” to cast the streamer as deceptive.
The key dividing line is whether the documentary is portrayed as broader accountability journalism or as a hostile rewrite.
Both sides agree on the lawsuit itself, but diverge along familiar entertainment-industry and media-power fault lines.
summary: Tyra Banks, 52, filed a lawsuit against Netflix on Saturday, accusing the streamer of defamation and manipulating interviews in the documentary Reality Check: Inside America's Next Top Model to support a false narrative.

Banks claims she provided a three-and-a-half-hour interview for the documentary, which producers cut to about 16 minutes; the lawsuit alleges this footage was reassembled to fabricate a narrative unrelated to what she actually expressed.

The lawsuit highlights an incident involving contestant Shandi Sullivan, claiming producers manipulated footage to suggest Banks forgot the event; unedited full footage shows Banks nodding and saying, "I do remember her story."

Seeking a jury trial, Banks requests an appropriate amount of punitive damages from Netflix, Blocks Holdings, EverWonder Studio, Netflix Music, and co-directors Mor Loushy and Daniel Sivan for false light and breach of contract.

Banks participated in the documentary because she believed viewers deserved a candid conversation about the show's legacy, successes, and shortcomings, the lawsuit states; Netflix has not publicly addressed the legal complaint.
title: Tyra Banks Sues Netflix over Portrayal in 'America's Next Top Model' Docuseries

bias: Left-Leaning coverage tends to personalize Vanessa Trump’s announcement as a candid health journey, tying it to family and celebrity context and, in some cases, Tiger Woods’ rehab return, while right-leaning pieces more often intensify the drama with words like “major,” “crucial new chapter,” “progress,” and “battle,” casting the update as a significant, hopeful development.
The center leans cleaner and more timeline-driven, stressing the “next stage” of treatment and even noting the surgery details are unclear.

Across both sides, the shared frame is recovery and support, but the divide lies in whether the story is presented as intimate human-interest, emotionally charged celebrity news, or a measured medical update.
summary: On Saturday, Vanessa Trump announced she is preparing to begin the "second stage" of her breast cancer treatment after spending four weeks recovering from surgery.
Trump revealed her breast cancer diagnosis in May and underwent an initial procedure as part of her treatment plan, focusing on healing since the announcement.
Offering encouragement to others, Trump wrote, "Sending love, strength, and hope to everyone fighting this battle," while her daughter Kai called her the "strongest person I know."
Throughout her recovery, Vanessa has maintained her relationship with Tiger Woods, who returned home to Florida recently to support her during this health journey.
Maintaining a positive outlook, Vanessa moves forward with her treatment, supported by her five children: Kai, Chloe, Donald III, Tristan, and Spencer.
title: Vanessa Trump to Start 'Second Stage' of Treatment for Breast Cancer
